title: Tales from the Arabian Nights
theme: Who You Want to Be vs. Who You Actually Are
description: A YA adaptation of One Thousand and One Nights exploring identity, wisdom, and the power of storytelling

chapters.title: The King Who Lost Faith
chapters.text:
The King Who Lost Faith

Long ago, in the city of Samarkand, there was a king named Shahriyar who had everything a man could want — wealth, power, a kingdom that stretched to the horizon, and a wife he trusted completely.
Then he discovered she had deceived him.
The discovery broke something in him. Not just his heart — something deeper, some belief he had held without knowing he held it: that the world was ordered, that loyalty existed, that the people closest to you were the people you could trust. He had built his life on this belief, and in one afternoon it crumbled to nothing.
He sent his wife away. And then, consumed by a rage that was also grief, he made a decision that would shadow his kingdom for a thousand nights: he would never trust again. Every evening he would take a new bride, and every morning he would send her away forever, before she had any chance to betray him.
His vizier — his closest advisor — carried out the orders, though his face grew more troubled with each passing month. The women of the kingdom began to hide their daughters. Fathers wept. Mothers packed their children off to distant relatives. The kingdom, which had once been prosperous and lively, grew hushed with fear.
The vizier had two daughters of his own. The elder was named Scheherazade.
Scheherazade was not like other young women of her time and place — or rather, she was like them in all the ordinary ways and unlike them in one extraordinary way: she had read everything. Every book in her father's library, every scroll in the palace archives, every tale and history and philosophical text she could lay her hands on. She had the kind of mind that held knowledge the way a river holds water — not as a collection of separate things but as a living current, everything connected to everything else.
She heard what was happening in the kingdom. She watched her father's face grow more haggard with each passing week. And she began to think.
'I want to go to the king,' she told her father one evening.
He stared at her as if she had suggested jumping from a cliff. 'Absolutely not.'
'I have a plan.'
'Your plan will get you killed.'
'My plan,' Scheherazade said calmly, 'might save a great many lives. Including mine.' She looked at him steadily. 'I know what I'm doing, Father. Trust me.'
He did not trust her plan. But he had never been able to deny her anything when she looked at him that way — with that particular combination of intelligence and certainty that had characterized her since she was old enough to form sentences.
He agreed, with a heart full of dread, to arrange the audience.
Scheherazade went to her younger sister Dunyazad that night and gave her careful instructions.
'When I am in the king's chambers,' she said, 'come to me before dawn and ask me to tell a story. Can you do that?'
'Why?' Dunyazad asked.
'Because I am going to tell him something he needs to hear. But I need to do it at the right moment, in the right way.' She paused. 'Stories are the only things that have ever changed anyone's mind about anything important. And this king's mind needs changing very badly.'
Dunyazad said she would do exactly as she was told.
Scheherazade lay awake that night, running through what she knew, assembling her plan. She was not unafraid. She was simply more determined than she was afraid, which is the closest most people ever get to courage.
In the morning, she went to the palace.

vocabulary.definition: Lying flat on the ground, usually in submission or despair; completely overcome by something.
Looking exhausted and unwell, especially from worry or suffering.
A firmly held belief or opinion; the feeling of being certain about something.

comprehension.question: The king punishes all women for one woman's betrayal. What logical error is he making?
options: He is being too lenient with the person who actually wronged him
He is treating an individual's action as evidence about an entire group — punishing everyone for what one person did
He is acting too quickly without gathering evidence
He is allowing his advisors too much influence over his decisions

comprehension.question: Scheherazade tells her sister that stories are 'the only things that have ever changed anyone's mind about anything important.' What does she mean?
options: That arguments and logic are useless
That people cannot be forced to change — they must be shown something and choose to respond to it, which is what stories allow
That the king is too uneducated for reasoned discussion
That she plans to entertain him until he forgets his anger

chapters.title: The First Night
chapters.text:
The First Night

The king received her without warmth. He was a large man who had been handsome once, before the grief and rage had settled into his face and hardened it. He looked at Scheherazade the way a man looks at something he has already decided about.
Scheherazade looked back at him with calm attention, the way she looked at a difficult text — with interest rather than fear.
She had brought Dunyazad with her, as she'd planned. Her sister sat quietly in the corner of the chamber, unobtrusive and watchful.
The evening passed. The king was not talkative. He asked her a few questions in the flat, mechanical way of a man going through motions he had long since stopped believing in. She answered simply and honestly, which seemed to surprise him slightly. He had perhaps expected flattery, or weeping.
Before dawn, exactly as instructed, Dunyazad spoke up from her corner.
'Sister,' she said, 'if you are not too tired, will you tell us a story? I have always loved your stories.'
The king, who had been looking out the window at the dark city, turned.
'Yes,' he said, after a moment. 'Tell a story.'
Scheherazade had been waiting for this. She gathered herself and began.
She told him the tale of a merchant who had, by accident, caused a genie's son to die, and who now faced the genie's wrath. The merchant begged for time to set his affairs in order before his death. The genie, to everyone's surprise — including perhaps his own — agreed. And as the merchant said his goodbyes, three old men appeared, each with a remarkable story to tell. The first old man began his tale...
And here Scheherazade stopped.
The room was absolutely silent. The candles had burned low. The sky outside the window was beginning to show the first grey edge of dawn.
'What happened next?' the king said.
He had spoken before he thought. She could see him realize it — the slight shift in his expression, the awareness that he had given something away.
'I could tell you tomorrow night,' Scheherazade said. She said it simply, without manipulation in her voice. As if it were merely a practical suggestion.
The king was quiet for a long moment.
'Tomorrow night,' he said.
She was brought back the following evening. And the evening after. Scheherazade had calculated very precisely — not just one story, but stories within stories, each tale opening like a door into another room, the thread of the narrative always continuing just past the point of resolution. The king could not kill her in the morning because he did not yet know what happened next. And he kept not knowing, night after night, because she kept not quite finishing.
But Scheherazade was not simply stalling for her life. She was doing something more deliberate than that.
Every story she chose, she chose for a reason. Every tale she told contained something she wanted the king to understand — about the consequences of cruelty, about the nature of trust, about what makes a ruler worthy of the people he rules. She was not entertaining him. She was educating him, night by night, one story at a time, in the only classroom where he could not refuse to sit: his own chamber, before dawn, with the thread of a tale left unfinished.
Dunyazad watched from her corner and marveled.
Her sister had walked into the lion's den with nothing but her voice and her mind.
So far, it was working.

vocabulary.definition: Done intentionally and carefully; fully thought through in advance.
To carefully adjust or measure something for a specific purpose.
Not attracting attention; staying in the background without being noticed.

comprehension.question: Scheherazade stops her story just before the resolution. Why is this the most important strategic decision she makes?
options: She is too tired to continue
She wants to see if the king is paying attention
The unfinished story creates a need in the king that only she can satisfy — he must keep her alive to find out what happens
She is afraid the ending will disappoint him

comprehension.question: The narrator says Scheherazade is 'not entertaining him' but 'educating him.' What is the difference, and why does it matter?
options: Entertainment is passive; education requires the listener to think and potentially change
Entertainment is for children; education is for adults
Entertainment is enjoyable; education is unpleasant
There is no real difference — education and entertainment serve the same purpose

chapters.title: Ali Baba and the Forty Thieves — Part One
chapters.text:
Ali Baba and the Forty Thieves — Part One

In a city at the foot of a mountain, Scheherazade began, there lived two brothers. Their names were Cassim and Ali Baba, and they had started life with the same nothing and arrived at very different somethings.
Cassim had married a wealthy woman and lived in a large house with servants and fine clothes and the comfortable certainty that came with money. He was not precisely unkind — he simply had the habit, common to people who have not struggled for a long time, of having forgotten what struggling was like.
Ali Baba had married for love, which had turned out to be a somewhat mixed financial strategy. He was a woodcutter. He woke early, worked hard, cut trees on the mountain, sold the wood in the market, and came home in the evening to a small house and a wife who was genuinely glad to see him. He was not rich, but he had the kind of contentment that Cassim, for all his wealth, did not quite have.
One morning Ali Baba was cutting wood on the mountain when he heard the sound of hoofbeats — many of them, coming fast. He climbed the nearest large tree and hid himself among the branches.
Forty men rode into the clearing below. They were hard-faced and well-armed, and they moved with the easy confidence of men accustomed to taking what they wanted. Their leader — a large man with a black beard and cold eyes — rode to the face of the mountain and spoke words that Ali Baba, from his tree, could just barely hear.
'Open Sesame.'
The rock face opened.
The forty men rode inside. The rock closed behind them.
Ali Baba gripped his branch and did not breathe.
After a long time — long enough that his legs were cramped and his heart had slowed from its first gallop — the rock opened again, and the forty men rode out. Their horses were now laden with saddlebags that clinked with unmistakable weight. Gold, Ali Baba thought. Or treasure of some kind. Whatever was in the cave was considerable.
The leader spoke again: 'Close Sesame.' The rock sealed itself, and the forty men rode away.
Ali Baba climbed down from his tree. He stood before the rock face for a long time.
He was an honest man. He had been an honest man his whole life, in the particular way of people for whom honesty is not a policy but simply the shape of how they move through the world. He did not take things that weren't his. He did not lie. He worked for what he had.
He was also, at this moment, a man standing before a door to unimaginable treasure, with the key in his memory, and no one watching.
He was in the tree, he told himself. He didn't steal the treasure. He simply found the door.
'Open Sesame,' he said.
The rock opened.
Inside was a cave so vast he could not see the far wall. It was stacked from floor to ceiling with bags of gold, bolts of silk and velvet, chests of jewels, silver plate, and things whose value he could not even estimate. Generations of theft, gathered here in the dark, waiting.
Ali Baba took what would fit on his donkeys — not all of it, not even a fraction of it. He took what seemed like enough. He brought it home and showed his wife, who sat down suddenly and had to be given water.
'We must bury it,' he said. 'Carefully. And we must not speak of it.'
His wife agreed. But she also wanted to know exactly how much they had, because she was a practical woman who liked to know where she stood. She went to borrow a measuring cup from Cassim's wife next door.
Cassim's wife lent the cup. But she was a curious woman, and she pressed a small piece of wax to the bottom of the cup before she gave it — just to see what it had been used to measure.
When the cup came back, there was a single gold coin stuck to the wax.
Scheherazade paused. The sky was lightening.
The king, she noted, had not moved in the last hour.
'Tomorrow night,' she said, 'I will tell you what Cassim's wife did with what she found.'

vocabulary.definition:
Part One
VOCABULARY
contentment  —  A state of happiness and satisfaction with what one has.
Acting with careful, selfish planning; concerned mainly with self-interest.
A very large quantity of something; more than enough.

comprehension.question: Ali Baba and Cassim started with the same nothing. What accounts for the difference in how they turned out?
options: Ali Baba worked harder than Cassim
Cassim married into money through luck
Their choices — Cassim chose security and status, Ali Baba chose love and acceptance — shaped who they became over time
Ali Baba had better luck than Cassim

comprehension.question: Ali Baba tells himself he 'didn't steal the treasure — he simply found the door.' What does this self-justification reveal?
options: That he is genuinely innocent of wrongdoing
That even honest people find ways to rationalize when something tempting is in front of them
That the treasure was genuinely ownerless and he had every right to it
That he plans to return it once he has paid his debts

chapters.title: Ali Baba and the Forty Thieves — Part Two
chapters.text:
Ali Baba and the Forty Thieves — Part Two

Cassim's wife showed her husband the gold coin, and Cassim went immediately to find his brother.
He was not pleased. He was not the sort of man who received news of another's good fortune with generosity, especially when that person was his less-successful brother. He demanded to know where the gold had come from, and when Ali Baba, after some hesitation, told him the whole story — the mountain, the cave, the forty thieves, the magic words — Cassim's eyes lit with a fire that had nothing to do with sharing.
'I will go tomorrow,' he said.
Ali Baba warned him to be careful, to take only a little, to come home before the thieves returned.
Cassim heard none of this. He was already calculating.
In the morning he rode to the mountain with ten mules and went to the cave. He said 'Open Sesame' and the rock opened and he went inside. He was not prepared for what he found — the scale of it, the sheer overwhelming abundance. He walked through it in a daze, touching things, counting things, trying to decide what to take first, then changing his mind, then changing it again.
Time passed. More time than he had intended.
When he finally decided he had loaded enough and went to the door to leave, he could not remember the magic words.
He said 'Open Wheat.' He said 'Open Barley.' He said the names of every grain he knew, every seed, every plant, because for some reason his mind had organized 'Sesame' alongside other things you grew in a garden, and it wouldn't come. He paced. He sweated. He could hear, distantly, the sound of hoofbeats.
The forty thieves found him when they returned.
We need not say more than that they were not merciful men.
When Cassim did not come home, his wife wept and then, being a practical woman in a difficult situation, went to Ali Baba. Ali Baba went to the cave and found what was left of his brother and brought him home with great sorrow and proper ceremony, and buried him respectfully.
And then the problem of the thieves.
They would know, the captain of the thieves understood, that someone else had discovered the cave. He sent a spy into the city to find out who. The spy was clever and diligent, and he traced the story back through a chain of small details to Ali Baba's house. He marked the door with a chalk mark so the captain could find it.
But Ali Baba had a servant — a woman named Morgiana, who was the most observant and resourceful person in the household. She noticed the chalk mark that evening. She did not know what it meant, but she knew that an unmarked mark on a door was never good news. She took a piece of chalk and put the same mark on every door in the street.
The captain's men came in the night and stood in the street, looking at forty identical marks, and did not know which house to enter.
Scheherazade smiled at the memory of telling this part. The king had leaned forward, she noticed. He was not a man who leaned forward at things.
Morgiana, she continued, was not done. When the captain tried again with a different disguise — an oil merchant, with large jars supposedly full of oil — Morgiana discovered the jars contained not oil but armed men, each one waiting for the signal to climb out and kill the household in their beds.
Morgiana built up the kitchen fire. She heated the real oil until it was scalding. And then, very quietly, she dealt with the situation.
The captain escaped. But his forty men did not.
'The rest,' Scheherazade told the king, 'I will tell you tomorrow — how the captain returned in a final disguise, and how Morgiana recognized him, and what she did.'

vocabulary.definition:
Part Two
VOCABULARY
diligent  —  Careful and persistent in work or effort.
The extent or range of something; how far something reaches.
Unable to be told apart; appearing exactly the same.

comprehension.question: Morgiana puts the same chalk mark on every door in the street. What quality does this action demonstrate?
options: She is trying to protect all her neighbors equally
She sees clearly and acts decisively on incomplete information — she doesn't know the mark's purpose but knows an unmarked mark is dangerous
She wants to confuse the thieves so they will give up
She is following instructions she received earlier

comprehension.question: Cassim forgets the magic words because his mind organized 'sesame' alongside other grains. What caused his fatal mistake?
options: He was too greedy and stayed too long
He was not as intelligent as Ali Baba
Both — his greed caused him to stay too long, and his panic blocked the word. His downfall was the combination of excess and losing his head under pressure
He was betrayed by someone who told the thieves he was there

chapters.title: Ali Baba and the Forty Thieves — Part Three
chapters.text:
Ali Baba and the Forty Thieves — Part Three

The captain of the thieves was not a man who gave up. He had built the greatest criminal enterprise in the region through persistence and intelligence, and he was not going to be defeated by a woodcutter's servant girl, however resourceful.
He disguised himself as a merchant of fine fabrics, rented a shop in the market near Ali Baba's house, and spent several months building a respectable reputation in the city. He was patient. He was charming. He became known as Khwaja Hassan — a pleasant man, reliable, fair in his dealings.
And eventually, as he had planned, he met Ali Baba's son, who introduced him to Ali Baba, who invited him to dinner.
Morgiana served the meal. She moved through the room with her usual quiet efficiency, filling cups, bringing dishes, observing everything.
It was when she was bringing the dessert that she saw it: a slight shift in the guest's posture when he thought no one was watching, the practiced stillness of a man controlling himself in proximity to a target. She had seen that quality before — in the oil merchant, in the chalk-marker, in the men who moved through the world like weapons held in check.
She knew him.
She said nothing. She finished serving the meal. And then she returned to the dining room in a dancer's costume — for she was a skilled dancer, among her other talents — and performed for the guests, as was the custom at celebrations.
As she danced, she drew closer to the guest. In her hand she held a dagger, which was also part of the performance. She spun toward him with professional grace and, in the moment that looked like part of the dance, she struck.
The captain of the forty thieves collapsed. His disguise fell away. And Ali Baba, staring at the man who had hunted his family for a year, finally understood the full scope of what Morgiana had done for his household.
'You saved my life,' he said. 'Three times at least, and once tonight.'
'Yes,' Morgiana said.
He gave her her freedom and his son's hand in marriage, which in that time and place was the highest reward he could offer. She accepted both, because she was a woman who knew her own worth and had never needed anyone to tell her what it was.
The captain's cave, its treasures now entirely Ali Baba's by default of having outlived everyone who knew about it, became the foundation of a generosity that Ali Baba exercised carefully for the rest of his life. He had not become rich by virtue. He had become rich by accident and survival. He understood the difference.
Scheherazade finished the tale and folded her hands.
The king was quiet for a moment.
'Morgiana,' he said.
'Yes.'
'She did more than Ali Baba to resolve the danger.'
'She did everything,' Scheherazade said simply. 'And she did it by paying attention — by seeing what others missed, and acting on what she saw.'
The king looked at her. It was a different look than he had given her on the first night — less flat, more present.
'What is the next story?' he said.
Scheherazade allowed herself, very quietly, to breathe.

vocabulary.definition:
Part Three
VOCABULARY
resilient  —  Able to recover quickly from difficult situations; not easily defeated.
A person or thing acting on behalf of another; a substitute.
The ability to judge people and situations well; good judgment.

comprehension.question: The king notices that Morgiana 'did everything' while Ali Baba mostly survived. What does this observation suggest about the story's actual hero?
options: Ali Baba is the hero because he found the cave
Heroes in stories are not always the people whose names are in the title — Morgiana's skill and attention drove every resolution
The story has no hero because all the characters have flaws
The captain of the thieves is the most impressive character

comprehension.question: Scheherazade says Morgiana's greatest weapon was 'paying attention.' How does this connect to the king's own situation?
options: The king should hire more observant servants
The king has been doing the opposite — reacting to one event without truly paying attention to what his reaction is producing
The king should be more suspicious of the people around him
Attention is only useful for people in danger

chapters.title: What Stories Do
chapters.text:
What Stories Do

Between tales, Dunyazad watched her sister with the careful attention of someone learning a new skill.
Scheherazade did not simply tell stories. She chose them with the precision of a physician choosing medicine — the right thing for the right condition, at the right time, in the right amount. Dunyazad had begun to see the pattern.
The tales about Morgiana were not about Morgiana. They were about what it meant to see clearly — to notice the chalk mark, to recognize the disguise, to act on what you knew rather than waiting for permission. The king was a man who had stopped seeing clearly. He had looked at one woman's betrayal and decided it was the truth about all women, about all people, about the world. Scheherazade was not arguing with him. She was showing him other evidence.
'You're teaching him,' Dunyazad said one morning, after the king had sent for his breakfast and they were alone for a few minutes.
'I'm offering him something to think about,' Scheherazade said. 'That's all stories can do. They can't change a person. They can only show a person something they hadn't considered. Whether they change is their own business.'
'But what if he doesn't change?'
Scheherazade was quiet for a moment. 'Then I will have told true stories to someone who needed to hear them, and done what I could with what I had.'
Dunyazad thought about this. 'Aren't you afraid?'
'Every night,' Scheherazade said. 'But fear is just information. It tells you the stakes. It doesn't tell you what to do.' She looked out the window at the city below — the market opening, the children going to school, the women at the wells. 'There are people in that city right now who are afraid because of what this king does. I am one person with one tool. I'm going to use it.'
This was Scheherazade as Dunyazad understood her: not a woman without fear, but a woman who had decided that something mattered more than the fear. Not reckless — she had thought carefully about the risk, had gone in with a plan, had built the plan on everything she knew. But determined.
The king sent a servant to say he would hear another story that evening.
Scheherazade straightened her dress and thought about what she would tell him next.
She had decided on Sinbad — the sailor who went to sea seven times and found the world stranger and larger than anyone at home had imagined. A man who was tested by everything the unknown could throw at him, and who kept going not because he was fearless but because he was curious, and curiosity, in the end, was stronger than fear.
It seemed, she thought, apt.

vocabulary.definition: Exact and accurate; carefully chosen for a specific purpose.
Acting without thinking about the consequences; careless about danger.
Done with full awareness and intention; carefully planned.

comprehension.question: Scheherazade says 'stories can't change a person — they can only show a person something they hadn't considered.' Why is this an important distinction?
options: It is a modest claim that understates what stories actually do
It places the responsibility for change on the listener, not the storyteller — Scheherazade cannot force the king to change, only offer him something to think with
It means Scheherazade does not actually believe her plan will work
It is a way of protecting herself if the king does not change

comprehension.question: Dunyazad observes that Scheherazade is 'not without fear' but is 'more determined than afraid.' What does this tell us about courage?
options: That courage means having no fear
That truly brave people are able to suppress their emotions completely
That courage is not the absence of fear but the choice to act despite it — when something matters more than the fear
That Scheherazade is unusually fearless by nature

chapters.title: Sinbad the Sailor — The First Voyage
chapters.text:
Sinbad the Sailor — The First Voyage

There was once, in the city of Baghdad, a poor porter named Sinbad who spent his days carrying loads for other people and his evenings wondering why some men were born to wealth and others to labor.
One afternoon, exhausted from the heat and the weight of the day, he sat down to rest outside a grand house from which came the sound of music and the smell of food that he would never taste. He muttered, to himself, about the injustice of the world.
A servant came out and told him the master of the house wished to speak with him.
The master was a man of middle age with an easy manner and a face weathered by years in places far from Baghdad. His name, he said, was Sinbad. Sinbad the Sailor.
The porter said, with some confusion, that this was also his name.
The sailor laughed. 'Then sit down, namesake, and eat, and I will tell you how I came to this house. Because I was not born to it. I was born exactly where you are sitting — in the dust, wondering why.'
This is what Scheherazade told the king, and this is what Sinbad the Sailor told his namesake:
He had inherited a modest sum from his father and spent it, as young men often do, on the pleasures of a city. When it was gone, he had nothing left but his wits and a berth on a merchant ship heading east.
The ship found an island, green and lovely, rising from the blue sea. The crew went ashore to cook and rest. They built fires on the beach. They were settling in with great contentment when the island moved.
It was not an island. It was a whale — the oldest, largest whale in the ocean, so ancient that soil had accumulated on its back and plants had grown there for centuries, so vast that it had passed for land to every ship that had ever stopped upon it. Now, disturbed by the fires, it dove.
Sinbad clung to a piece of driftwood and watched his ship sail away without him. He floated for a day and a night, through cold water and dark, telling himself at every moment that he was not dead yet, that not-dead-yet was enough to work with.
He washed up on a real island. On the island he found the king's horses being watered, and the king's grooms, and eventually the king himself — a generous man who gave Sinbad a position in his court and treated him well.
He might have stayed. It was a comfortable life, and he had earned a comfortable life.
But one day a merchant ship stopped at the island. And on the deck of the ship, Sinbad recognized his own goods — the cargo he had loaded in Baghdad, which he had been convinced was lost.
The captain had kept everything safe and sought the owner. He returned it all to Sinbad, every item accounted for. Sinbad sold it for a good price and sailed home to Baghdad considerably richer than he had left.
'The first lesson the sea taught me,' Sinbad the Sailor told his namesake, 'was that the world is larger and stranger than anything you can imagine from one city. The second was that honesty has a long reach — it comes back to you when you least expect it.'
Scheherazade paused. Dawn was an hour away.
'Tomorrow,' she said, 'I will tell you about Sinbad's second voyage, which was stranger still.'

vocabulary.definition:
The First Voyage
VOCABULARY
berth  —  A place on a ship for sleeping; also a job or position on a vessel.
The quality of being honest and having strong moral principles; doing the right thing even when no one is watching.
Protective care from a higher power; timely preparation that helps you in difficulty.

comprehension.question: The ship's captain kept Sinbad's goods safe and returned them in full. What principle does this demonstrate, and why does Sinbad call it his 'second lesson'?
options: That merchant ships are generally trustworthy
That honesty has consequences — good ones. The captain's integrity came back to benefit Sinbad years later in a way neither could have predicted
That Sinbad was lucky to sail with a good captain
That it is always wise to insure your cargo

comprehension.question: The 'island' that turns out to be a whale is one of the most famous images in the Arabian Nights. What does it represent thematically?
options: The danger of sea travel
The idea that what seems solid and reliable can shift without warning — the world is stranger and less stable than it appears from home
The importance of researching a destination before you land
The cruelty of nature toward human travelers

chapters.title: Sinbad the Sailor — The Second and Third Voyages
chapters.text:
Sinbad the Sailor — The Second and Third Voyages

The second voyage taught Sinbad something harder to learn: that you cannot always choose your circumstances, only what you do within them.
He had gone home from the first voyage intending to stay. Baghdad was comfortable, he was wealthy, there was no rational reason to go to sea again. But the sea had gotten into him — the largeness of it, the sense that around every horizon was something he hadn't imagined yet. One morning he found himself at the docks, watching the ships, and by evening he had booked passage on another merchant vessel.
They landed on an island that seemed uninhabited. The crew went ashore. Sinbad wandered too far from the beach, lay down in the shade of a tree, and fell asleep. He woke to silence. The ship was gone — the crew had not noticed him missing.
He climbed a tall tree to get his bearings and saw, in the distance, something enormous and white. He made his way to it and found an egg — perfectly round, the size of a house, smooth as marble. A roc's egg. The roc was a bird so large it fed its young on elephants.
When the roc returned to its nest and settled over the egg, Sinbad made a calculation. The bird was enormous, yes. But it flew. And he had nowhere else to go.
He tied himself to the roc's leg with his turban. In the morning the roc took flight and Sinbad went with it, up through clouds, across distances he could not measure, until the bird descended into a valley and he untied himself and tumbled free.
The valley was filled with diamonds. Enormous ones, scattered across the ground like pebbles. It was also filled with snakes large enough to swallow a man whole.
He had found the Valley of Diamonds, which merchants had tried to reach for generations without success. The method they used — he had heard of it — was to throw pieces of meat into the valley from above, so that eagles would swoop down and carry the meat back up with diamonds stuck to it, which the merchants would then collect.
Sinbad strapped diamonds to a piece of meat and lay on top of it and waited.
The eagle came.
He went home with a fortune in diamonds, which was remarkable. What he told his guests he valued more was this: that in the worst situation he could imagine — alone, in a valley of diamonds and serpents, with no apparent way out — he had thought clearly and found the door.
The third voyage brought the island of the giant — a creature so large it had to find its crew members by touch, like a man choosing fruit at a market. Three sailors did not survive that night. Sinbad and the remaining crew built a raft and escaped in the dark while the giant slept, pursued to the water's edge by the giant's family, who threw boulders after them.
'Three voyages,' the sailor told his namesake. 'Three times I nearly died. And each time I came home. Do you know why?'
'Luck,' the porter suggested.
'Partly. But mostly: I did not stop thinking. Even when everything was terrible, I looked at what was in front of me and asked what could be done with it. The man who keeps thinking keeps living, more often than not.'
Scheherazade folded her hands.
The king said nothing for a moment. Then: 'He went back four more times?'
'Four more times,' she confirmed. 'Each one stranger than the last.'

vocabulary.definition:
The Second and Third Voyages
VOCABULARY
improvise  —  To create or do something on the spot without preparation, using whatever is available.
A difficult, unpleasant, or embarrassing situation with no easy way out.
The state of being calm and in control of oneself, especially in difficult situations.

comprehension.question: In the Valley of Diamonds, Sinbad is trapped with no apparent way out. What enables him to escape?
options: He finds a hidden passage in the valley walls
A rescue party comes looking for him
He remembers a story about merchants and eagles and adapts it to his situation — he uses knowledge and lateral thinking rather than force
He waits for the roc to return and ties himself to it again

comprehension.question: Sinbad's formula for survival — 'the man who keeps thinking keeps living' — is tested across every voyage. What does this suggest about the quality he values most?
options: Physical strength and endurance
Luck and divine favor
Mental clarity under pressure — the ability to assess a situation and act thoughtfully even when afraid
Wealth and resources

chapters.title: Sinbad the Sailor — Later Voyages
chapters.text:
Sinbad the Sailor — Later Voyages

The fourth voyage showed Sinbad what it meant to be truly foreign.
Shipwrecked again — the sea was not kind to a man who returned to it so often — he and a handful of survivors washed ashore in a country whose people were unlike any he had met. They were hospitable. They gave the survivors food and care. But Sinbad noticed, with growing unease, that the food they gave was very rich, meant to be eaten in large quantities, and that the locals watched them eat with something that was not quite ordinary friendliness.
He ate sparingly. The others ate everything offered.
The others grew slow, their minds softening, their sharpness dulling. Sinbad stayed alert, watchful, increasingly certain he understood what was happening.
He escaped into the forest. He found a road. He followed it to a kingdom whose ruler was wise and just, who heard Sinbad's story and gave him a position at court. He made himself useful, he made himself known, he made himself — carefully and gradually — someone the king trusted and valued.
And then he found the king's ship and came home.
The fifth voyage confronted him with the Old Man of the Sea — a creature that looked, at first, like a helpless old man who needed to be carried across a stream. Sinbad carried him. The old man wrapped his legs around Sinbad's neck and would not let go, directing him where to walk, sleeping on his shoulders at night, making himself impossible to dislodge.
For months Sinbad carried him, increasingly exhausted, trying and failing to find a way to remove him.
The solution came from fermented grape juice. Sinbad made wine from wild grapes and offered some to the old man, who had never tasted it. The old man drank, became unsteady, loosened his grip. Sinbad threw him off and ran.
The sixth voyage brought him to an island of extraordinary abundance — spices and jewels and wonders — but no inhabitants. It turned out to be the kingdom of Serendib, whose wise king received him with such generosity that Sinbad agreed to carry a message and gifts back to the Caliph of Baghdad.
The Caliph was so delighted with the gifts and Sinbad's account that he sent Sinbad back to Serendib with gifts in return. Sinbad became, without quite intending it, a diplomat — a bridge between two rulers who had never known the other existed.
'And the seventh voyage?' the porter asked.
Sinbad smiled. 'The seventh was the strangest of all. I was captured by a people who had never seen a ship, who thought the sea was the edge of the world. I taught them to build boats. I showed them there was more beyond what they could see.' He paused. 'And then I came home for the last time, because I finally understood what I had been going to sea to find.'
'What?'
'That the world is enormous and that I am not the center of it. That there are people living full lives in places I had never imagined, with wisdom I had never considered. That the man who travels with an open mind comes home richer than the man who travels with his mind closed, even if he brings back less gold.'
Scheherazade let the room settle.
'Tomorrow,' she said, 'a different kind of story. One about a boy who was given everything — and almost lost it by not understanding what everything was worth.'

vocabulary.definition:
Later Voyages
VOCABULARY
incentive  —  Something that motivates or encourages a person to do something.
A person who manages relations between countries or groups, using tact and skill.
Having a limited or narrow outlook; only concerned with your own area or group.

comprehension.question: On the fourth voyage, Sinbad eats sparingly while the others eat everything offered. What quality saves him that the others lack?
options: He is not as hungry as the others
He has dietary restrictions
He pays close attention to his surroundings and notices something is wrong with the situation before it becomes too late to act
He has been in this situation before and recognizes it

comprehension.question: Sinbad says the real treasure of his seven voyages was learning 'the world is enormous and I am not the center of it.' How does this lesson connect to the 7th grade theme?
options: It shows that travel is more valuable than education
It shows that who you think you are — the center of things — is often not who you actually are in the larger world; humility is part of knowing yourself truly
It means Sinbad became less ambitious over time
It is a warning against leaving home too often

chapters.title: The Merchant and the Genie
chapters.text:
The Merchant and the Genie

Before she came to Aladdin, Scheherazade told the king a shorter tale — one that had been the beginning of all the tales, the frame within the frame.
A merchant was traveling between cities when he stopped to eat dates under a palm tree. He threw the date stones aside as he ate, casually, without watching where they fell. When he finished and rose to continue his journey, a genie appeared before him — vast and terrible, its form half-smoke and half-solid, its voice like a desert wind.
'You have killed my son,' the genie said.
The merchant did not understand. He had killed nothing.
'The date stone you threw,' the genie said. 'It struck my son as he passed, and he died of it.'
This was, by any ordinary measure, absurd. A date stone. An invisible son, struck down by careless eating. But genies did not live by ordinary measures, and this one's grief was real and its rage was real, whatever the human mind thought about the likelihood.
'I am going to kill you,' the genie said.
The merchant begged for time. He had a family. He needed to settle his affairs. Could he have one year?
The genie, to everyone's surprise, agreed. Perhaps it was the merchant's willingness to return and face justice — so many people, given the chance to run, simply ran. This man said he would come back.
He came back. He sat under the same palm tree and prepared himself.
Three old men arrived, each leading an animal. Each old man had a story of extraordinary strangeness — a wife turned into a gazelle by magic, brothers turned into dogs — and each one offered his story to the genie as a bargain: if my story is remarkable enough, spare this merchant one-third of his fate.
The genie found all three stories remarkable. The merchant lived.
'Why do you tell me this story?' the king asked. He had learned to ask this question. In the early nights he had simply listened; now he was looking for the intention beneath the tale.
Scheherazade considered her answer.
'Because of the merchant,' she said. 'He threw a date stone without watching where it went. He caused harm without meaning to — without cruelty, without malice, simply without paying attention. And the consequence was enormous.' She paused. 'And because the genie agreed to listen before acting. Because when it listened, it was moved. It found three lives interesting enough to preserve one.' She met the king's eyes. 'A ruler who listens is a different ruler than one who does not.'
The king was quiet for a long time.
'Tell me about Aladdin,' he said.

vocabulary.definition: Not resulting from deliberate planning; accidental.
Extreme anger; fierce and violent rage.
The quality of being in proper balance or relation to something else; a response sized appropriately to what caused it.

comprehension.question: The genie's son was killed by a carelessly thrown date stone. What does this scenario suggest about harm and intention?
options: That accidents are not morally serious because they are not intentional
That harm caused without malice is still harm — carelessness has real consequences even when there is no cruelty behind it
That genies are unreasonably sensitive
That the merchant should have stayed on the road

comprehension.question: Scheherazade tells the king this story and then says 'a ruler who listens is a different ruler than one who does not.' What is she really saying to him?
options: That he should hold more formal hearings
That the genie made a mistake by not listening first
That he has been like the genie — acting from rage before listening, letting his anger determine outcomes that listening might have changed
That stories about genies teach practical political lessons

chapters.title: Aladdin — The Lamp
chapters.text:
Aladdin — The Lamp

Aladdin was not, to begin with, the hero of his own story.
He was a boy in a Chinese city — lazy, cheerful, good-natured in the way of people who have never been asked to be anything else. His father had died when he was young. His mother worked her fingers to the bone. Aladdin played in the streets with boys whose names he had mostly forgotten by evening.
Then a man came who said he was his uncle.
Aladdin had no uncle. But this man had the right names, the right details, the right tears at the memory of a brother long dead, and Aladdin's mother, who was too tired and too lonely to question too closely, believed him.
The man was not his uncle. He was a sorcerer from a distant country who had spent years searching for a particular lamp that lay in a particular underground cave that could only be entered by a particular boy with a particular destiny — and by precise and arcane calculation, that boy was Aladdin.
He could not enter the cave himself. That was the rule of the magic, laid down long ago by powers the sorcerer understood only partially. He needed the boy.
He took Aladdin on a journey. He dressed him well and fed him well and treated him with every apparent kindness. Aladdin, who had never been treated particularly kindly by anyone outside his mother, was charmed.
At the mouth of the cave, the sorcerer gave Aladdin a ring — for protection, he said. He told him to go down into the cave, past the trees of jeweled fruit, past the pools of water, to a lamp sitting on a ledge at the far end. Touch nothing else. Take only the lamp. Bring it up to him.
Aladdin went down. He was frightened — the cave was dark and strange and the air hummed with a kind of pressure he felt in his chest. But he found the lamp, a plain, battered thing that looked worth nothing. He picked it up. He filled his pockets with the jeweled fruit along the way, because they were beautiful and he was still, at heart, a boy who picked up pretty things.
When he reached the cave's mouth and asked the sorcerer to help him up, the sorcerer reached for the lamp first.
Aladdin hesitated. Something in the sorcerer's face — the sudden sharpness, the focus — made him pull back.
'Help me out first,' Aladdin said.
'Give me the lamp first,' the sorcerer said.
Neither moved. Aladdin saw, in that moment of stillness, the truth of the situation: the sorcerer did not care about him at all. He was a tool, a key, a means to an end. The moment the lamp changed hands, his usefulness was over.
'No,' Aladdin said.
The sorcerer, in his rage, sealed the cave.
Aladdin sat in the dark with the lamp and the jeweled fruit and no way out, and thought, for the first time in his life, very hard.

vocabulary.definition:
The Lamp
VOCABULARY
arcane  —  Understood by only a few; mysterious and secret.
Designed to be useful or practical rather than attractive; focused on purpose over appearance.
Keeping careful watch for possible danger or difficulty.

comprehension.question: When the sorcerer reaches for the lamp before helping Aladdin out of the cave, Aladdin hesitates. What has Aladdin seen that he hadn't seen before?
options: That the lamp is more valuable than he thought
That the sorcerer is in a hurry
The sorcerer's true priorities — in that moment of focus, the mask slips and Aladdin sees that he is a tool, not a nephew
That the cave is beginning to close

comprehension.question: Aladdin refuses to give up the lamp even though he is now sealed in the dark with no way out. What does this decision tell us about him?
options: He is stubborn and doesn't think about consequences
He has made a calculation — keeping the lamp is his only leverage; surrendering it means certain death, while keeping it means uncertain danger
He doesn't realize the danger he is in
He is angry at the sorcerer and wants revenge

chapters.title: Aladdin — The Genie
chapters.text:
Aladdin — The Genie

He rubbed his hands together in the dark, partly for warmth, partly from anxiety, and in doing so he rubbed the ring the sorcerer had given him.
A genie appeared. Small, compared to the genies in stories — or perhaps the cave made everything seem smaller. It said it was the genie of the ring and asked what was required of it.
Aladdin asked to be taken home.
He appeared in his own street, outside his mother's door, still holding the lamp.
His mother wept. Then she asked what had happened. Then she looked at the lamp he had brought, which was old and dirty, and thought that perhaps she could sell it if she cleaned it up. She began to polish it.
The genie of the lamp was not small.
It filled the room and spilled out the window and its voice was like a door slamming in an empty hall. It said it was the servant of whoever held the lamp and asked what was commanded.
Aladdin's mother fainted.
Aladdin took the lamp and asked for food.
There was food — on plates of silver, in quantities that could have fed the street.
This was the beginning of Aladdin's education, which was not the education he had received from the sorcerer, nor the education he had received in the streets, but something harder: the education of a person who has been given too much, too fast, without the foundation to use it well.
He was not immediately wise about it. He was sixteen years old and suddenly in possession of a genie of unlimited power. He made mistakes. He asked for things that seemed urgent and turned out to be useless. He asked for things he thought would make him happy and found they only made him aware of what he still lacked.
What he lacked, he gradually understood, was not things. It was judgment.
The princess entered the story the way most things entered Aladdin's story — unexpectedly. He saw her once, briefly, in the street, and was so struck by her grace and intelligence that he told his mother he intended to marry her.
His mother looked at him.
'The princess,' she said carefully, 'is the daughter of the Sultan.'
'Yes,' Aladdin said.
'We are not—'
'I know what we are,' Aladdin said. 'And I know what the lamp can do. But I want to earn this. Not just have the genie make it happen.'
His mother looked at him for a long moment. He had never said anything quite like that before.
'Then we start,' she said, 'with the jewels from the cave.'
They went to the Sultan's court. Aladdin did not use the lamp. He brought the jeweled fruit from the cave — real treasure, genuinely his, not conjured. He made his case through his mother, who was terrified but determined. He waited.
The Sultan was impressed enough to hear more.
Scheherazade paused. The king's eyes were on her fully, the same eyes that had looked at her on the first night with flat indifference.
'Tomorrow,' she said, 'what Aladdin built — and nearly lost.'

vocabulary.definition:
The Genie
VOCABULARY
conjure  —  To produce something as if by magic; to summon or call forth.
Respectful submission to the judgment of another; yielding to someone else's opinion.
The ability to judge wisely and carefully; good judgment about what matters.

comprehension.question: Aladdin makes mistakes with the lamp at first — asking for things that don't bring happiness. What is he learning about the relationship between power and judgment?
options: That the genie is limited in what it can actually do
That having power doesn't mean knowing how to use it well — judgment must be developed separately from the tools available
That wealth always disappoints eventually
That he should ask for wisdom first

comprehension.question: Aladdin says he wants to 'earn' the princess, not just have the genie make it happen. Why is this a significant moment in his development?
options: He is worried the genie's magic won't work on royalty
He wants the princess to like him genuinely
He is beginning to understand the difference between having something and deserving it — and choosing the harder path that leads to real rather than conjured worth
He is afraid the Sultan will investigate how he got his wealth

chapters.title: Aladdin — What He Built and Nearly Lost
chapters.text:
Aladdin — What He Built and Nearly Lost

The palace Aladdin built rose overnight.
He had resisted using the lamp for a long time — longer than anyone who knew him as a boy would have predicted. He had courted the princess slowly, earned the Sultan's cautious respect through patience and genuine gifts, and spent two years simply proving that he was more than the street boy he had been. But the Sultan finally said he needed a palace, a home worthy of a princess, before the wedding could be arranged.
Aladdin looked at the empty lot across from the Sultan's own palace and told the genie what he wanted: not the grandest palace in the world, but one that was made with craft and beauty, where every room had something to recommend it, where the garden was designed by someone who understood gardens, where the kitchens were large enough to feed the whole city on feast days.
The genie built it by morning.
Aladdin and the princess were married. He was, by all accounts, a good husband — attentive, honest, willing to listen. The people of the city loved him because he fed the poor from his kitchens and was visible in the streets, not hidden behind palace walls. He had not forgotten where he came from. This was the best thing about him.
The sorcerer had not forgotten him either.
The sorcerer had survived his own rage, recovered from his humiliation, and spent the intervening years planning. He had determined that the lamp was still in play — the palace, the marriage, all of it screamed the genie's work. He came back to the city in disguise and waited.
His opportunity came when Aladdin was away on a hunt.
He moved through the city buying old lamps, calling in the street: new lamps for old. A merchant's game, ordinary enough. He came to the palace. The princess did not know the story of the lamp — Aladdin had not told her, which was his single significant error of judgment. She saw an old lamp sitting in the prince's room. She thought it was, well, an old lamp. She traded it for a new one.
The sorcerer had what he had always wanted.
That night he used it. The palace vanished. The princess vanished with it — transported to the sorcerer's home in a distant country, where he proceeded to make her life extremely unpleasant while waiting for Aladdin.
Aladdin came home from the hunt to an empty lot and a Sultan demanding his daughter back.
He still had the ring.
He asked the ring-genie to take him to the princess. It could not undo the magic of the greater lamp, but it could transport him. He appeared in the room where the princess was kept and they had, very briefly, a reunion that was also a planning session.
The princess hated the sorcerer. The princess was also, it turned out, someone who had spent the last three days thinking carefully about the situation while Aladdin was presumably finding his feet.
'There is wine he likes,' she said. 'He has it every evening.'
She needed nothing more than a small packet of powder, pressed into the wine, to complete the plan.
The sorcerer drank his wine that evening and did not wake up. Aladdin retrieved the lamp. He brought his wife and his palace home.
The king asked Scheherazade what Aladdin had learned.
'That power without trust is a door you leave open for someone else to walk through,' she said. 'He didn't tell the princess about the lamp because he was afraid she would think less of him — would see the magic instead of the man. But his silence nearly cost him everything. The truth told earlier would have been the better armor.'

vocabulary.definition:
What He Built and Nearly Lost
VOCABULARY
vulnerability  —  A weakness or exposed point that can be used against you; openness to harm.
Not able to be undone or changed; permanent.
The quality of being open and honest; frankness.

comprehension.question: Aladdin did not tell the princess about the lamp because he feared she would 'see the magic instead of the man.' What does this fear cost him?
options: The lamp itself, which is stolen while he is away
His silence created a vulnerability that the sorcerer exploited — the secret meant to protect him became the door through which everything was lost
The princess's trust, which he never fully regains
His reputation in the kingdom

comprehension.question: The princess had been planning her own escape for three days before Aladdin arrived. What does her preparation reveal about her character?
options: She did not trust Aladdin to rescue her
She is resourceful and refuses to be only a victim — even in captivity she is thinking and acting, not waiting
She is more intelligent than Aladdin
She had help from servants inside the sorcerer's house

chapters.title: The Fisherman and the Genie
chapters.text:
The Fisherman and the Genie

There was a fisherman, Scheherazade told the king, who was poor in the way that good men sometimes are — through no fault of their own but simply the particular arrangement of the world that gives some people everything and other people the daily work of getting by.
He cast his net four times each day. The first three casts of this particular day brought him a dead donkey, a jar of sand, and an armful of broken pottery. He was considering the theology of a universe that gave a hardworking man broken pottery when the fourth cast brought up a copper jar, sealed with lead, stamped with the seal of Solomon.
He knew what this might mean. Everyone knew the stories. But he was practical about it — he broke the seal and opened the jar, because a sealed jar found in a net is worth opening regardless of what's inside.
There was smoke. Then there was a genie — vast and terrible, older than the fisherman had words for.
The genie said that it had been imprisoned in the jar for eighteen hundred years. For the first four hundred years, it had planned to give whoever freed it every treasure in the world. For the second four hundred years, it had planned to grant three wishes. For the next four hundred years, it had planned to grant one wish. After that it had grown so enraged by confinement that it had decided to kill whoever freed it, by whatever painful means came to mind.
The fisherman was having, in sum, a very bad fishing day.
He thought quickly. 'I believe,' he told the genie, 'that you were really in that jar. But I'm afraid I can't believe you're as large as you appear. That jar is quite small.'
The genie found this insulting. It said of course it had been in the jar. The fisherman said that seemed impossible given its current size. The genie said it could fit back in the jar very easily and proceeded, to demonstrate the point, to do exactly that.
The fisherman sealed the jar.
'Now,' he said to the sealed jar, 'we can discuss terms.'
The genie, from inside the jar, made various threats. The fisherman waited. Eventually the genie agreed to cooperate — specifically, to lead the fisherman to a lake where fish of extraordinary color lived, which would make his fortune, in exchange for his freedom.
The fisherman thought carefully about this offer, evaluated the genie's incentives, decided that a genie who had just been outwitted would prefer to repair its reputation through legitimate cooperation rather than risk further humiliation, and opened the jar.
The genie kept its word. The fisherman became prosperous.
'The genie's mistake,' Scheherazade told the king, 'was assuming its power meant it didn't need to think. It was stronger, older, and more terrible than the fisherman by every measure. And yet the fisherman survived because he used what he had — his wits — while the genie had stopped needing to.'
She paused.
'Power that goes unexamined becomes stupidity,' she said quietly. 'The genie sat in its rage for a thousand years and did not notice it had become predictable.'
The king said nothing for a long time.
Then: 'Tell me another.'

vocabulary.definition: Power or influence that can be used to achieve a purpose; using a small force to produce a larger effect.
Excessive pride in one's appearance or abilities; an inflated sense of one's own importance.
Behaving in a way that can be expected; easy to foresee because of habit or pattern.

comprehension.question: The fisherman tricks the genie back into the jar by challenging its pride. What weakness does this exploit?
options: The genie's poor memory
The genie's need to prove itself — its vanity makes it do exactly what the fisherman needs it to do
The genie's obligation to obey whoever holds the jar
The genie's fear of being destroyed

comprehension.question: Scheherazade says 'power that goes unexamined becomes stupidity.' What does she mean, and how does this apply to the king?
options: That powerful people eventually make bad decisions
That power without reflection leads to predictable patterns that can be exploited — and a king who acts from habit and rage has stopped thinking just as the genie did
That the genie should have been smarter about its situation
That fishermen are cleverer than genies

chapters.title: The Tale of the Three Apples
chapters.text:
The Tale of the Three Apples

There was a murder mystery, of a kind, that Scheherazade told the king on a cold night.
A chest had been found in the river by a fisherman and brought to the Caliph. Inside the chest was a woman, young and beautiful and dead, wrapped in a costly robe. The Caliph was deeply disturbed by this — that someone had been killed and hidden this way in his city, in his river, as if justice were not a thing that could reach them.
He told his chief minister that if the killer were not found within three days, he would hang the minister in the killer's place.
The minister searched. He found nothing. He came back at the end of three days prepared to die, and a young man presented himself and confessed.
The young man said the woman was his wife, whom he had loved dearly. He had been ill, and she had gone out to find him medicine, and she had returned with three apples. He had eaten one. He recovered. He saw a slave in the street carrying a single apple, exactly like his wife's, and asked where it came from. The slave said he'd received it from his girlfriend, who had told him her husband was a fool who'd bought three apples especially for her, and here was one.
The husband had gone home to his wife in a rage and done what he had done, and then discovered that all three of his wife's apples were still in the house. The slave had lied. The slave had gotten the apple some other way, had invented the story to boast, and the husband had believed him without question and acted without waiting.
He had killed his wife because of a lie he had not bothered to verify.
The minister wept. The Caliph wept. The young man stood there with the particular stillness of a man who had already condemned himself more thoroughly than any court could manage.
The Caliph did not hang him. He had three children who had already lost their mother.
The slave was found. He was brought before the court. He had never imagined a casual lie told in the street could reach this far, could touch something so real and so permanent.
'Is this a story about the slave?' the king asked.
'It is a story about the husband,' Scheherazade said. 'He was not a villain. He was not cruel by nature. He acted on incomplete information and the weight of his own jealousy and suspicion, and the action was irreversible.' She met the king's eyes steadily. 'When a decision cannot be undone, the moment before it requires everything you have. All the patience. All the doubt. All the willingness to ask one more question before you act.'
The king looked at her for a long moment.
'You are very direct,' he said. 'For a storyteller.'
'I am a storyteller,' Scheherazade said. 'Stories are the most direct thing I know.'

vocabulary.definition: Not able to be recovered or undone; permanently lost.
The process of checking whether something is true or accurate.
A series of events each caused by the previous one, like a waterfall.

comprehension.question: The husband acted on one lie without verifying it and caused irreversible harm. What is Scheherazade demonstrating about the relationship between anger and decision-making?
options: That lies are more dangerous than any other wrongdoing
That anger compresses time — it creates urgency that collapses the space between feeling and action, eliminating the crucial pause where verification could happen
That husbands should not trust slaves
That jealousy is always destructive

comprehension.question: Scheherazade says 'the moment before an irreversible decision requires everything you have.' What does she mean by 'everything'?
options: All available evidence and witnesses
All your patience, doubt, willingness to wait, and capacity to ask one more question before acting permanently
All the power and resources at your disposal
All the legal protections available to you

chapters.title: The Prince of the Black Islands
chapters.text:
The Prince of the Black Islands

There was a young king, Scheherazade told the king, who discovered that his wife had been deceiving him.
He handled the discovery badly. He did not think about what it meant or what it asked of him — he simply acted, and his action was cruel, and the cruelty produced a curse that turned his kingdom to stone and his own legs to marble from the waist down, so that he could neither leave his throne nor govern his people.
He sat there for years, half-stone, unable to move, the kingdom around him still and silent as a held breath.
A traveling king found him and asked what had happened.
'My wife deceived me,' the young king said. 'So I punished her.'
'And the kingdom?' the traveling king asked, gesturing at the stone streets, the stone people, the stopped world.
'Was caught in between,' the young king said.
He seemed to hear, saying it, what it meant.
The traveling king went to find the wife. It was a strange and tangled story — she had her own reasons, her own griefs, her own terrible choices, and none of it was simple. But the traveling king was a practical man. He untangled it. He did what needed to be done.
The curse lifted. The kingdom woke. The young king's legs were restored.
'What did he do with his kingdom?' the king asked. 'After?'
'He governed it more carefully,' Scheherazade said. 'He had sat for years watching what happened when a ruler acted from rage rather than judgment. He had seen his own people frozen, unable to live their lives, because of a moment in which he could not hold himself in check.' She paused. 'He did not forget it. That was the gift of the curse, if a curse can have gifts: he could not pretend it hadn't happened. He had sat in it for years.'
The king in the chamber was very still.
Dunyazad, in her corner, did not look up from her sewing. But her hands had stopped moving.

vocabulary.definition: Inability to move or act; being frozen in place by fear, indecision, or circumstance.
Something that is affected as a side effect of an action aimed at something else.
A time when the consequences of past actions must be faced; a moment of truth.

comprehension.question: The curse turns the kingdom to stone as a consequence of the king's cruelty. What does this image represent?
options: The supernatural power of the queen's magic
A ruler's cruelty does not stay contained — it spreads outward and freezes the whole kingdom in its effects
The importance of not angering powerful magicians
The queen's desire for revenge against innocent people

comprehension.question: The young king is restored but has spent years watching his kingdom frozen. Why might this prolonged suffering be, as Scheherazade says, 'the gift of the curse'?
options: It gave him time to plan improvements to his kingdom
He could not escape or deny what his action had caused — he sat in the consequences long enough to truly understand them, which makes him less likely to repeat them
It made him more sympathetic to his subjects' suffering
It taught him patience

chapters.title: The Tale of Judar
chapters.text:
The Tale of Judar

There was a young man named Judar, Scheherazade said, who had two brothers and a mother and very little else, and who went on a journey that would have broken most people.
His brothers were not good people. They took what Judar earned, invented stories to excuse themselves, borrowed money they had no intention of repaying, and when Judar tried to hold them accountable, they went to their mother and wept, and their mother believed them because she was a woman who loved her sons and found it easier to doubt Judar than to doubt the other two.
Judar left.
He became, through a sequence of events involving a Moroccan magician and an enchanted treasure and a fish made of gold that swam in the sea, immensely wealthy. He went home. He divided his wealth with his brothers. He asked nothing in return except honesty going forward.
His brothers thanked him, took the money, and immediately began planning how to get the rest.
They poisoned him at a feast.
It was, Scheherazade noted, not a surprise to anyone who had been paying attention. The pattern was visible from the first page. The only person who didn't see it — or wouldn't see it — was Judar, because he had decided, at some point, that loving people meant trusting them regardless of evidence, and he could not separate love from trust.
'Is this a story about being foolish?' the king asked.
'It is a story about the difference between love and willful blindness,' Scheherazade said. 'Judar loved his brothers. That was real and it was good. But love does not require you to ignore what is plainly in front of you. He saw who they were and decided to love them anyway, which he had every right to do. But he also gave them power over him, over and over, which was the choice that destroyed him.' She was quiet for a moment. 'You can choose to love people who have betrayed you. But wisdom asks you to also choose carefully where you place your trust. These are not the same decision.'
The king was quiet for longer than usual.
'You have been telling me stories,' he said, 'about people who act from rage without thinking, and about people who trust without discernment, and about people who see clearly and survive because of it.'
'Yes,' Scheherazade said.
'Why?'
She looked at him directly. 'Because I think you are a man who knows what he lost, but has confused it with something larger. One person betrayed you. You have punished the world.' She paused. 'I do not say this to accuse you. I say it because you are not a foolish man, and a man who is not foolish should eventually be able to see the distance between what happened and what he has done in response.'
The room was very quiet.
'That is not a story,' the king said.
'No,' Scheherazade agreed. 'That is what I think.'

vocabulary.definition: Deliberate; done with full awareness and intention, especially of something wrong.
Showing a lack of experience or judgment; too ready to trust without sufficient evidence.
A repeated sequence of events or behaviors that reveals something about a person or situation.

comprehension.question: Scheherazade says the outcome of Judar's story 'was not a surprise to anyone paying attention.' What does this suggest about the relationship between love and clear-sightedness?
options: That loving someone means you cannot see them clearly
That Judar should have stopped loving his brothers
That love does not require blindness — Judar could have loved his brothers and still seen their pattern clearly, and made different decisions about what to trust them with
That Judar was less observant than most people

comprehension.question: This is the first story where Scheherazade speaks directly to the king — 'one person betrayed you, and you have punished the world.' Why does she choose this moment to drop the story format?
options: She has run out of stories
She is angry and can no longer control herself
After sixteen nights of showing him things through stories, she judges he is ready to hear it plainly — the stories have done their work and the direct truth is now the right tool
She wants to see how he will react to being challenged

chapters.title: The Tale of Qamar and Budur
chapters.text:
The Tale of Qamar and Budur

Now Scheherazade told a tale of two people who were destined for each other but kept being separated by circumstances, by misunderstanding, by the sheer bad timing of a world that does not arrange itself for human convenience.
Qamar was a prince who refused to marry, not from cruelty but from a genuine inability to imagine meeting someone he actually wanted to spend his life with. Budur was a princess in the same situation, on the other side of the world. The genies, who had some interest in the matter, arranged for them to be transported in their sleep to the same room, where they could see each other and then be returned before either woke.
They each woke with a memory they couldn't be sure was real. The memory was of someone — a face, a presence — that seemed more real than anyone they had actually met.
Both of them searched. The story took years — adventures and disguises and misunderstandings and reunions and separations and a great deal of traveling through places neither of them knew. There were moments when they were so close they could have reached each other, if either had known. There were moments when they believed the other was dead.
They found each other in the end. This was not the interesting part.
The interesting part was what they became during the years of searching.
Qamar became a man who had seen the world, who had worked alongside ordinary people and understood what ordinary life required of people. He had been shipwrecked, imprisoned, employed as a gardener, trusted with secrets, and tested in ways that no princely education would have provided. He returned to his kingdom knowing things about governing it that he could not have known before.
Budur became a woman who had governed a kingdom in disguise — she had passed as a king, had made decisions, had learned that authority carried weight and that using it required care. When her disguise was eventually discovered, the people of the kingdom asked her to stay on as queen, because she had been a better ruler than their previous kings.
'They each became more,' Scheherazade said, 'because they were separated. The separation was not only loss. It was also making.'
She let this sit for a moment.
'The difficult things that happen to us,' she said, 'are not only punishment. They are also education — if we are willing to learn from them rather than only suffer them.'
She did not look at the king when she said this. She looked at her hands.
But she felt him hearing it.

vocabulary.definition: The occurrence of pleasant or valuable things by chance; fortunate accidents.
To create something strong through sustained effort; to develop over time.
Occurring as a minor part of something else; not the main purpose.

comprehension.question: Budur governs a kingdom in disguise and the people ask her to stay on as their ruler. What does this reveal about the nature of good governance?
options: That women make better rulers than men
That good governance is about what you actually do, not who you are by birth or title
That people will follow anyone who seems confident
That Budur was always destined to be a queen

comprehension.question: Scheherazade says the years of separation were 'making' as well as loss. How does this connect to the 7th grade theme about who you actually are versus who you want to be?
options: It means suffering always makes you a better person
The gap between who they were and who they became was crossed through difficulty — they became their actual selves by being tested, not by having things go smoothly
It means wanting something is not enough — you have to work for it
It is a comforting idea that has no practical application

chapters.title: The Ebony Horse
chapters.text:
The Ebony Horse

A mechanical horse carved from ebony was presented to a great king as a gift. It was the most remarkable object any of them had ever seen — a horse that could fly, that responded to hidden mechanisms in the saddle, that could carry a rider to any destination in the world.
The king's son asked to ride it.
The inventor who had built the horse had not yet finished explaining how it worked. The prince had not yet heard the part about how to make it descend. He only knew how to make it rise.
He rose.
He rose very, very high.
He spent a long time in the sky above the city, discovering through experimentation what the inventor had not yet told him: there was a second mechanism, a pin on the right shoulder of the horse, that controlled the descent. He found it eventually. He landed in a foreign kingdom, in the garden of a palace, in the middle of the night.
In the garden was a princess.
Her name was Shams al-Nahar — Morning Sun. She was reading in the garden by lamplight because she was a person who read when she could not sleep, which was often. She was not frightened by the prince falling out of the sky on a mechanical horse, which told him something about her.
He said hello. She said hello. They talked for the rest of the night.
He flew home in the morning. He came back the next night. He came back the night after that. This went on for some time — a secret courtship conducted in the hours of darkness, between two people who both understood that what they were doing was not conventional and both found they did not care.
Eventually the princess's father found out. This created several difficulties involving imprisonment and disguise and the mechanical horse changing hands more than once and the prince having to rescue the princess from a foreign king who had ideas about what to do with a princess found in a flying horse.
The prince prevailed. They married. The inventor was rewarded.
But what Scheherazade chose to emphasize was not the adventure.
'He came back,' she said. 'Every night, he found the mechanism to descend, which was harder than the mechanism to rise. He chose, deliberately, the harder thing — to return to the earth, to the conversation, to the person.' She paused. 'Most people find rising easy. The descent is the discipline.'
The king looked at her. He had been doing this more often — looking at her as if recalibrating something.
'You mean something by that,' he said.
'I mean several things,' she said. 'I leave you to choose which one applies.'

vocabulary.definition: A system of parts working together; the means by which something is done.
Training that develops self-control and the ability to do what is required even when it is difficult.
Given or done in return; mutual.

comprehension.question: The prince must find the descent mechanism every time he flies — it requires deliberate effort each time, unlike the ascent. What does this symbolize?
options: That flying is more dangerous than it appears
That going up is natural and easy; choosing to come back down — to return to earth, to relationship, to reality — requires active effort and discipline
That the horse was not perfectly designed
That the prince needed more training before flying

comprehension.question: Scheherazade says 'most people find rising easy; the descent is the discipline.' She leaves the king to decide which meaning applies to him. What meanings might she intend?
options: Only one meaning — the king should literally travel less
